coredata.dc:title: Ultrasound-cavitation-enhanced drug delivery via microbubble clustering induced by acoustic vortex tweezers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Ching-Hsiang
Huang, Elaine
Lo, Wei-Chen
Yeh, Chih-Kuang
coredata.dc:description: The application of acoustic vortex tweezers (AVT) in conjunction with ultrasound (US) cavitation pulses presents a promising noninvasive approach for the delivery of high concentrations of therapeutic agents. This methodology facilitates the aggregation of drug-loaded microbubbles (MBs) into clusters, which are subsequently destroyed to release their contents. Nevertheless, prior investigations have not thoroughly examined the resonance frequency and cavitation activity of MB clusters, critical factors that could enhance the efficiency of payload release. Theoretically, the resonance frequency of an MB cluster is expected to approximate that of a single large bubble of comparable size, thus being significantly lower than that of the individual MBs constituting the cluster. Accordingly, this study aims to optimize the release of payloads from AVT-trapped MB clusters, which measure 15 to 40 μm (mean radius: 24.7 μm) in size, by employing US at their resonance frequency of 100 kHz, henceforth referred to as “on-resonance US.” In this investigation, MBs were loaded with the model drug DiI, resulting in the formation of DiI-MBs, which were then clustered utilizing AVT. On-resonance US excitation was subsequently applied to enhance the release of the drug payload. The dimensional characteristics of the DiI-MB clusters formed via 3-MHz AVT were measured to determine the range of resonance frequencies. Concurrent optical and acoustic analyses were conducted to evaluate the size, oscillation dynamics, and cavitation activity of the DiI-MB clusters in response to on-resonance US excitation. Additionally, the payload release from these clusters was quantitatively assessed. Our results indicate that significant oscillations of individual DiI-MB clusters commenced at a pressure of 44 kPa during 100 kHz US excitation. Further quantitative experiments demonstrated that the synergistic combination of AVT and 100-kHz US at 65 kPa significantly enhanced the payload release efficiency to 93 %. This efficiency surpassed that achieved with either method independently, with increases of 1.8-fold relative to AVT alone and 2.3-fold compared to 100-kHz US alone. The acoustic analyses revealed the onset of inertial cavitation at 44 kPa, which strongly correlated with payload release efficiency (R2 = 0.78). These findings underscore the potential of our proposed methodology in monitoring and enhancing the efficiency of drug release.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acoustic vortex tweezer
Microbubbles
Ultrasound
Inertial cavitation
Targeted drug delivery
Low-frequency ultrasound

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Estimating the resonance frequency of AVT-trapped DiI-MBs clusters
Measurement and analysis of the DiI-MB cluster oscillation dynamics
AVT waveform characterization and microscopy observations
Oscillations and cavitation activity of DiI-MB clusters in response to on-resonance US
Quantitative analyses of payload release from DiI-MB clusters induced by on-resonance US

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Science and Technology Council, Taiwan (grant number NSTC 111-2221-E-007-019-MY3, 113-2221-E-007-022-MY3, and 113-2923-E-007-004-MY2 to C.K.Y.; NSTC 113-2636-E-006-002 to C.H.F.).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the concept of this study. DiI dye was embedded in the lipid shell of MBs to mimic a small-molecule lipophilic drug. The size of the AVT-trapped DiI-MBs clusters was evaluated to estimate their resonance frequency. Next, these clusters excited by on-resonance US at 100 kHz were monitored using high-speed microscopy imaging and a PCD transducer to analyze the oscillation dynamics and acoustic disruption threshold, respectively. Additionally, the release of fluorescent DiI dye from the MBs clusters was monitored and measured to quantify their payload release.

(a) The experimental setup. (b) Timing diagram for monitoring DiI-MBs clusters by bright-field high-speed microscopy imaging during 100-kHz US excitation. (c) Timing diagram for capturing payload release from DiI-MBs clusters by fluorescence high-speed microscopy imaging during 100-kHz US excitation. (d) Experimental setup for quantifying payload release from the DiI-MBs clusters during 100-kHz US excitation. (e) Timing diagram for quantifying payload release from DiI-MBs clusters by collecting the sonicated sample during 100-kHz US excitation.

(a) The illustration structure of AVT transducer. (b) Measured acoustic field of the AVT in the x-z plane. (c) Left: measured acoustic field of the AVT in the x-y plane. Right: lateral acoustic pressure profile of the AVT in the x-y plane. (d) Bright-field, fluorescence, and merged images of the DiI-MBs. (e) The size distribution of DiI-MBs. (f) Dynamics of DiI-MBs cluster formation by the AVT. (g) Size distribution of the DiI-MBs clusters, N = 151.

(a) Bright-field high-speed microscopy images and radius–time curves of the oscillation dynamics of the DiI-MBs clusters before and during 100-kHz US excitation at different acoustic pressures. Greyscale high-speed microscopic sequence images depicting radial oscillations of DiI-MBs clusters. The corresponding time intervals are indicated by red and blue shades on the radius-time curves. (b) Oscillation responses of the DiI-MBs clusters during 100-kHz US excitation at different acoustic pressures. Each box plot shows the median, first and third quartiles, and range. (c) ICD signal intensities for AVT-only, 100-kHz US-only, and AVT + 100-kHz US groups measured by the PCD. (d) Payload release dynamics of DiI-MB clusters by fluorescence high-speed microscopy imaging during 100-kHz US excitation at different acoustic pressures. Red arrow: the ejection of DiI payload. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Payload release efficiency of the DiI-MBs clusters for different US-based configurations at different acoustic pressures: (1) AVT only group, (2) 100-kHz US group, (3) AVT + 100-kHz US group, (4) 3-MHz US group, (5) ARF + 3-MHz US group, (6) AVT + 3-MHz US group, (7) ARF + 100-kHz US group, and (8) sonication-only group. *p < 0.01 vs. 100-kHz US group, #p < 0.01 vs. AVT only group. (b) Correlation between ICD and payload release efficiency in the AVT + 100-kHz US group.
US transducers and the applied acoustic parameters used in this study.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This article is part of a special issue entitled: ‘Bioeffects of ultrasonic’ published in Ultrasonics Sonochemistry.

head.ce:title: Ultrasound-cavitation-enhanced drug delivery via microbubble clustering induced by acoustic vortex tweezers

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biomedical Engineering and Environmental Sciences

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: C.-H. Fan and E. Huang equal contribution.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The application of acoustic vortex tweezers (AVT) in conjunction with ultrasound (US) cavitation pulses presents a promising noninvasive approach for the delivery of high concentrations of therapeutic agents. This methodology facilitates the aggregation of drug-loaded microbubbles (MBs) into clusters, which are subsequently destroyed to release their contents. Nevertheless, prior investigations have not thoroughly examined the resonance frequency and cavitation activity of MB clusters, critical factors that could enhance the efficiency of payload release. Theoretically, the resonance frequency of an MB cluster is expected to approximate that of a single large bubble of comparable size, thus being significantly lower than that of the individual MBs constituting the cluster. Accordingly, this study aims to optimize the release of payloads from AVT-trapped MB clusters, which measure 15 to 40 μm (mean radius: 24.7 μm) in size, by employing US at their resonance frequency of 100 kHz, henceforth referred to as “on-resonance US.” In this investigation, MBs were loaded with the model drug DiI, resulting in the formation of DiI-MBs, which were then clustered utilizing AVT. On-resonance US excitation was subsequently applied to enhance the release of the drug payload. The dimensional characteristics of the DiI-MB clusters formed via 3-MHz AVT were measured to determine the range of resonance frequencies. Concurrent optical and acoustic analyses were conducted to evaluate the size, oscillation dynamics, and cavitation activity of the DiI-MB clusters in response to on-resonance US excitation. Additionally, the payload release from these clusters was quantitatively assessed. Our results indicate that significant oscillations of individual DiI-MB clusters commenced at a pressure of 44 kPa during 100 kHz US excitation. Further quantitative experiments demonstrated that the synergistic combination of AVT and 100-kHz US at 65 kPa significantly enhanced the payload release efficiency to 93 %. This efficiency surpassed that achieved with either method independently, with increases of 1.8-fold relative to AVT alone and 2.3-fold compared to 100-kHz US alone. The acoustic analyses revealed the onset of inertial cavitation at 44 kPa, which strongly correlated with payload release efficiency (R2 = 0.78). These findings underscore the potential of our proposed methodology in monitoring and enhancing the efficiency of drug release.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Combining ultrasound (US) with drug-loaded microbubbles (MBs) is a promising strategy for noninvasively delivering intravascular drugs as tumor treatments in different animal models [1–3]. US excitation will induce cavitation in the drug-loaded MBs, in turn permeabilizing nearby vessel walls and releasing the payload [4–9]. However, the efficiency of the drug delivery achieved has varied. A key limiting factor is that the high velocity of the blood circulation (0.4 to 5 mm/s in capillaries and venules greatly reduces the concentration of the released payload at the lesion site [10]. This impedes the retention of drugs and limits drug concentration in the targeted region as MBs retention decreases with increasing shear rate [11,12]. Besides, the non-uniform flow velocities and inertial forces within blood vessels would induce most MBs traveling along the centerline of the vessel [7], making it challenging for MBs to keep in contact with endothelial cells for a prolonged time. The high doses of drug-loaded MBs necessary to overcome this high blood flow to ensure complete tumor control are usually accompanied by serious side effects, such as systemic cytotoxicity, cavitation overdose, and unpredicted gas embolism. Therefore, the development of a noninvasive approach that can increase the local drug concentration and enhance the active release of payloads from drug-loaded MBs into target sites would improve the overall efficacy of disease management.

Several US-based configurations have been proposed for the noninvasive spatial manipulation and concentration of drug-loaded MBs. One example is the application of acoustic radiation forces (ARFs) to push MBs to the distal wall via primary Bjerknes forces [13,14]. Dayton et al. reported that sonicating circulating MBs at 5 MHz, 800 kPa, and a pulse repetition frequency (PRF) of 10 kHz generated ARFs in a mouse arteriole, which reduced the velocity of MBs from 7.5 mm/s to less than 1 mm/s by directing them toward the vessel wall [14]. Additionally, MBs would reversibly aggregate due to secondary Bjerknes forces, potentially enhancing the concentration for targeted deliveries. Although a stronger ARF could counteract higher flow velocities in the bloodstream, increasing the magnitude of these forces might trigger biochemical responses such as cytoskeletal reorganization due to a high mechanical index [36].

It has been demonstrated previously that single-beam acoustic tweezers can precisely trap micron-sized particles inside the ex vivo [15]. However, trapping micron-sized particles requires single-beam acoustic tweezers to operate at several tens of megahertz (up to 40 MHz). The poor tissue penetration of such high-frequency US would significantly limit the in vivo applications of single-beam acoustic tweezers. To address the obstacles of current techniques, we previously constructed acoustic vortex tweezers (AVT) capable of spatially focusing drug-loaded MBs into clusters, which improved the local drug concentrations by up to 1.7-fold and 5.9-fold in dorsal vessels of mice and the cerebral vasculature of rats, respectively [16,17]. However, no previous study has evaluated drug release from AVT-trapped drug-loaded MBs clusters in response to external US excitation.

Efficiently triggering payload release from drug-loaded MBs clusters requires knowledge of the resonance frequency and cavitation activity of the clusters in response to US. Under low-intensity US, MBs undergo steady oscillations (stable cavitation), resulting in small payload release [18–21]. In contrast, high-intensity US induces large-amplitude asymmetric oscillations in MBs, eventually leading to their violent collapse (inertial cavitation). Such inertial cavitation can fully release the MBs payload, but it also generates strong mechanical stresses that can damage nearby tissues [22,23]. Additionally, the oscillations of MBs are enhanced upon exposure to US at their resonance frequency, which increases the payload release. However, previous studies using high-speed microscopy imaging showed that the clustering of MBs can alter their echogenicity, resulting in these clusters being shielded and resistant to high-intensity US that would be expected to destroy individual MBs [24]. Kokhuis et al. similarly reported that individual MBs and MBs-bound stem-cell clusters have resonance frequencies of 1.3 and 0.6 MHz, respectively [25]. Therefore, the relationships between resonance frequency, cavitation activity, and the payload release of AVT-trapped drug-loaded MBs clusters remain to be fully elucidated.

The aim of this study was to investigate a synergistic approach using AVT-trapped drug-loaded MBs clusters and US at their resonance frequency (called “on-resonance US” below) to increase the cavitation activity and oscillations so as to maximize payload release (Fig. 1 ). Initially, the highly lipophilic fluorescent dye DiI (1,1'-dioctadecyl-3,3,3',3'-tetramethylindocarbocyanine perchlorate; 961.3 Da) was embedded in the lipid shell of MBs to mimic a small-molecule lipophilic drug. The sizes of the AVT-trapped DiI-MBs clusters were evaluated to estimate their resonance frequency. These clusters excited by on-resonance US were recorded using high-speed microscopy imaging and passive cavitation detection (PCD) to analyze their oscillation dynamics and acoustic disruption threshold, respectively. Additionally, the release of the fluorescent DiI dye from the MB clusters was measured to quantify their payload release. Finally, a comparative analysis of payload release efficiency was performed by using US pulses at a frequency far from the resonance frequency of the clusters (called “off-resonance US” below) along with ARFs, as commonly applied in US-mediated drug delivery.

The MBs were fabricated using the thin-film hydration method, encapsulating perfluoropropane (C3F8) gas as the inner core inside the lipid shell [26]. The lipid shell consisted of DPPC (1,2-dipalmitoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine; catalog numbers: 63–89-8), DSPG (1,2-distearoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoglycerol; catalog numbers: 200880–42-8), and DSPE-PEG-5000 (1,2-distearoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolamine-N-[methoxy(polyethylene glycol)-5000]; catalog numbers: 474922–77-5) (Avanti Polar Lipids, AL, USA). The DiI dye (catalog numbers: 41085–99-8; Sigma-Aldrich, MO, USA), which contains a polar “head” (indocarbocyanine group) and two hydrophobic alkyl “tails,” was embedded within the lipid shell of the MBs. To prepare the DiI-MBs, DPPC, DSPG, DSPE-PEG-5000, and DiI were mixed (weight ratio of 10:4:4:0.25 mg), and dissolved in 1 mL of chloroform. The chloroform was then evaporated by a Rotavapor (Model R‐210, Büchi Labortechnik, AG, Switzerland) to generate a lipid film. The lipid film was rehydrated with glycerol-phosphate buffered saline (PBS) (glycerol-to-PBS ratio 0.1:20). The mixture was degassed, refilled with C3F8 gas, and vigorously shaken. During this shaking process, the phospholipid materials and DiI dye would self-assemble to encapsulate C3F8, forming DiI-MBs. The DiI-MBs solution was centrifuged at 2,000 g for 2 min and subnatant solution was replaced into fresh glycerol-PBS to remove excess lipids and DiI dye.

The DiI-MBs were observed using a microscope (IX-71, Olympus, Melville, NY, USA) equipped with a 40 × water immersion objective to examine the morphology. The size of the DiI-MBs was measured using a Coulter counter (Multisizer 3, Beckman Coulter, CA, USA). The loading efficiency of DiI on DiI-MBs was measured by a reverse method. The DiI-MBs were first destructed by a sonicator (model 2510, Branson, NY, USA) and then mixed with methanol to achieve a uniform suspension. The fluorescence intensity of DiI was subsequently measured using a fluorescence reader (Spark 10 M, Tecan, Männedorf, Switzerland) with excitation/emission wavelengths at 532/577 nm to estimate the amount of loaded DiI. The loading efficiency of DiI was estimated by (mass of loaded DiI/ mass of total DiI) × 100 %.

The in-house AVT transducer was constructed following the method outlined by Lo et al. [16]. A 6.5-mm aperture with a focusing depth of 20 mm was precisely drilled through the center of a 3-MHz lead zirconate titanate piezo composite (HIFU Piezo, Hubei Hannas Tech, China) to ensure an unobstructed light path for optical experiments. The electrode on the convex side was laser diced into four independent elements, with each section connected to an individual coaxial wire. A common ground wire was attached to the concave side. Finally, the transducer was sealed using epoxy within a resin cylinder that had been printed with a masked stereolithography 3D printer (Saturn 2, Elegoo, Shenzhen, China).

The experimental design of this study included six key components to comprehensively characterize the behavior of MBs clusters: (1) quantification of MBs cluster size, (2) estimation of cluster resonance frequency, (3) measurement of resonance frequency under US excitation, (4) evaluation of cavitation activity, (5) assessment of payload release, and (6) comparative analysis. An integrated acousto-optical system was employed to simultaneously measure the size, oscillation dynamics, and acoustic destruction threshold of DiI-MBs clusters subjected to 100-kHz US excitation (GS100-25, The Ultran Group, PA, USA).

As illustrated in Fig. 2 a, the experimental setup consisted of a 100-kHz US excitation transducer, an AVT transducer, a PCD system, and a high-speed camera (FASTCAM SA4, Photron, Tokyo, Japan) coupled to the microscope equipped with a 10 × water immersion objective, all housed within a water tank. The tank was filled with deionized and degassed water maintained at 37 °C to simulate in vivo conditions. The AVT transducer was precisely aligned along the optical axis of the microscope and focused on a 200-μm cellulose tube (Spectra/Por® In Vivo Microdialysis Hollow Fibers, Spectrum Laboratories, CA, USA), which served to aggregate the DiI-MBs into clusters. Concurrently, the 100-kHz excitation transducer was aligned with the tube to deliver focused US pulses. The PCD system incorporated a 1-MHz US transducer (Olympus NDT Inc., MA, USA), oriented perpendicularly to the AVT transducer, to detect emitted acoustic signals.

Detailed operational parameters for all transducers are summarized in Table 1 . The AVT transducer was driven by two synchronized arbitrary-function generators (AFG3022C, Tektronix, Beaverton, OR, USA) to transmit US waves with phase differences of 0π, 0.5π, 1π, and 1.5π radians. The acoustic field and pressure generated by this transducer were calibrated using a hydrophone (HGL-0085, ONDA, Sunnyvale, CA, USA) in distilled and degassed water at 25 °C. For comparative studies, the AVT transducer was replaced with an in-house 3-MHz US focused transducer to deliver acoustic radiation force (ARF) pulses.

Similarly, the 100-kHz excitation transducer was powered by an arbitrary-function generator, with acoustic pressures calibrated using a low-frequency hydrophone (TC4035, Teledyne RESON, Slangerup, Denmark) under identical water tank conditions. In the comparative analysis, this transducer was substituted with a custom 3-MHz focused US transducer to generate off-resonance US pulses.

The PCD signals acquired via the 1-MHz focused US ransducer were amplified using a pulser/receiver (5072PR, Olympus NDT, MA, USA) and digitized at a 25 MHz sampling rate by an oscilloscope (MSO46, Tektronix, OR, USA). This setup enabled precise detection and characterization of the acoustic emissions associated with MBs cluster activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimating the resonance frequency of AVT-trapped DiI-MBs clusters
ce:section.ce:para:
A previous study found that on-resonance US could efficiently induce the volumetric vibration and cavitation of MBs [27,28], and so the present study investigated the resonance frequency of the DiI-MB clusters necessary to optimize their payload release. The resonance frequency of a single MB depends on its radius [29]:
(1)
f =
1
2 π r
3 γ
P A
ρ
where γ is the polytropic coefficient, PA is the ambient pressure, ρ is the density of the surrounding fluid, and r is the radius of a single MB.

The modified formula for the resonance frequency of a cluster of bubbles considers the effects of interactions between multiple bubbles on the overall resonance characteristics. The collective oscillations of the MBs within the cluster often result in spectral broadening of the resonance frequency [30]. Due to the substantial difference in compressibility between air and water, the presence of any air can significantly alter the compressibility of their mixture. When a spherical MB cluster is treated as an effective medium, its resonance frequency can be determined using the formula developed by Carey and Fitzgerald [30]:
(2)
f R
=
1
2 π
R b
3 γ
P A
χ ρ
where fR is the resonance frequency of the MB cluster, Rb is the cluster radius, γ is the specific heat ratio of C3F8 (assumed to be 1.03), PA is the ambient pressure (assumed to be 105 Pa),
χ
is the fraction of air in the fluid (assumed to be 1 for an air bubble with radius R), and ρ is the density of the surrounding fluid (assumed to be 1000 kg/m3).

To estimate the resonance frequency of the DiI-MB clusters, they were diluted to a concentration of 5 × 108 MBs/mL and trapped by AVT (165 kPa, 100 cycles, and 6-Hz PRF) in the cellulose tube. The radius distribution of the DiI-MB clusters was measured by bright-field high-speed microscopy imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement and analysis of the DiI-MB cluster oscillation dynamics
ce:section.ce:para: The oscillations of DiI-MBs clusters during on-resonance US (100 kHz) excitation were monitored by acquiring a series of bright-field high-speed microscopy images of the clusters before and after the excitation in order to construct the radius–time curve (Fig. 2b). The frame rate was set to 360 kfps for acquiring 300 μs of data. The DiI-MBs were diluted to a concentration of 5 × 108 MBs/mL and trapped by AVT (165 kPa, 100 cycles, and 6-Hz PRF) in the cellulose tube. The 100-kHz US excitation was applied as a single pulse, 30 cycles (300 μ s of pulse length) and at acoustic pressures of 10, 28, 44, 58, and 65 kPa.

The acquired images were then processed using the MIJ Java package in MATLAB (MathWorks, Natick, MA, USA). Briefly, the images were first converted to binary images, holes were filled, and circles were fitted to the data. The radii of the fitted circles were then measured over time.

The acoustic signals generated by the DiI-MBs clusters and subsequently captured by the PCD system during 100-kHz US excitation were subjected to analysis using MATLAB. A Hanning window was employed to mitigate spectral leakage, and the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) was applied to convert the signals into the frequency domain. Within this framework, the inertial cavitation dose (ICD) was quantified in the range of 0.9 to 1.0 MHz [31]. This parameter serves as a key metric, reflecting the broadband emissions and mechanical energy released during the violent collapse of MBs, which are essential indicators of non-linear acoustic activity and therapeutic efficacy [32,33].

ICDPBS was obtained from a degassed PBS solution as a control, while ICDMBs was obtained during the DiI-MBs-clusters experiments, and the final ICD was then calculated as ICD = ICDMBs – ICDPBS. The DiI-MBs were diluted to a concentration of 5 × 108 MBs/mL and trapped by AVT (165 kPa, 100 cycles, and 6-Hz PRF) in the cellulose tube. The 100-kHz US excitation was applied as a single pulse, 30 cycles, and at acoustic pressures of 10, 28, 44, 58, and 65 kPa.

The payload release dynamics of DiI-MBs clusters during 100-kHz US excitation was monitored and quantified by the high-speed microscopy with fluorescence imaging and the fluorescence reader, respectively. For monitoring the payload release dynamics, a 532-nm laser (SDL-532–1000 T, Dream Lasers Technology, Shanghai, China) was used as the light source to enhance the signals from the released payload. The frame rate was set to 40 kfps for acquiring 1200 μs of data (Fig. 2c). The DiI-MBs were diluted to a concentration of 5 × 108 MBs/mL and trapped by AVT (165 kPa, 100 cycles, and 6-Hz PRF) in the cellulose tube. The 100-kHz US excitation was applied as a single pulse, 60 cycles (600 μs of pulse length), and at acoustic pressures of 10, 44, and 65 kPa.

The quantified payload release from DiI-MBs clusters during 100-kHz US excitation was determined by analyzing the fluorescence intensity of DiI collected from the outlet fluid. For these experiments the 200-μm cellulose tube was replaced with a PE-50 tube since DiI might leak from the walls of a cellulose tube, which have a typical pore size of 13 kDa. DiI-MBs at a concentration of 5 × 108 MBs/mL were injected into the PE-50 tube at a velocity of 3 mm/s using an injection pump (Fig. 2d).

The AVT were activated at 1-s intervals for 7.5 min (Fig. 2e). This time was used to confirm that the MBs were trapped within the focal volume of the AVT. The 100-kHz US excitation was applied at a PRF of 0.22 Hz, 1000 cycles, and acoustic pressures of 10, 28, 44, 58, and 65 kPa for 7.5 min. Fluid samples totaling 250 μL were acquired and centrifuged at 500 × g for 2 min in order to separate the released DiI (in the aqueous subnatant) from intact DiI-MBs (in the supernatant). The aqueous subnatant was extracted using an insulin syringe. Finally, the fluorescence intensity of the released DiI was quantified using a microplate fluorescence reader at excitation and emission wavelengths of 532 and 577 nm, respectively. The fluorescence intensity from control treatments (I Control) in which DiI-MBs passed through the PE-50 tube without US excitation was used for background subtraction.

Eight experimental groups were used: (1) control group, (2) AVT-only group, (3) 100-kHz US-only group, (4) AVT + 100-kHz US group, (5) ARF + 100-kHz US group, (6) ARF + 3-MHz US group, (7) AVT + 3-MHz US group, and (8) sonication-only group. Direct sonication treatment served as the 100 % benchmark (I Sonication) for data normalization. The ARF was applied at a frequency of 3 MHz, PRF of 6 Hz, 100 cycles, and an acoustic pressure of 165 kPa. The 3-MHz US excitation was applied at a PRF of 6 Hz, 100 cycles, and an acoustic pressure of 1000 kPa as the comparison for the effects of off-resonance US excitation. The payload release efficiency was calculated as
(3)
Payload release efficiency =
I
T r e a t m e n t
-
I
C o n t r o l
I
S o n i c a t i o n
-
I
C o n t r o l
×
100%
where I Control is the fluorescence intensity of the control sample (without US excitation), I Treatment is the fluorescence intensity in the experimental group (after US excitation), and I Sonication is the fluorescence intensity of DiI-MBs excited by the sonicator alone.

All data are presented as mean ± standard-deviation values obtained in experiments conducted independently at least three times. Designated comparisons between two groups were performed using a two-tailed unpaired Student’s t-test. For other comparisons of unequal variances, one-way ANOVA was performed followed by the Games-Howell post-hoc test with SPSS software (version 20, IBM SPSS Statistics version 20.0, IBM Corporation, Armonk, NY, USA). Statistical significance of the data was designated using asterisks in the figures as follows: p < 0.05 (*).

ce:section.ce:section-title: AVT waveform characterization and microscopy observations
ce:section.ce:para: The illustration structure of AVT transducer and the transmitted acoustic field of AVT is shown in Fig. 3 a. Applying phase shifts from 0 to 2π radians resulted in the generation of a ring-shaped intensity peak at a depth of 20 mm with a low-amplitude center in the x-z plane (Fig. 3b). This phenomenon was caused by destructive interference forming a ring structure. The beam pattern in the x-y plane displayed notable pressure peaks on both sides of the central axis in the x direction (at y = 0 mm), with a 0.76-mm gap between them (Fig. 3c). This gap exhibited a steep pressure gradient that resulted in inward lateral forces toward the center, which aided in trapping the MBs.

We then assessed whether the DiI-MBs could be trapped by the AVT through microscopy observations. Fig. 3d indicates overlapping of the contours of the DiI-MBs and the distribution of red fluorescence from DiI, indicating that DiI had been successfully loaded into the shell of the MBs (the loading efficiency was 38.8 ± 2.9 %) (Fig. 3d). The mean size of the DiI-MBs was approximately 1.2 ± 0.5 μm (Fig. 3e). When the AVT was activated, the suspended MBs rapidly spiraled clockwise around the beam axis due to phase shifts. These MBs subsequently aggregated in the center to form a DiI-MBs cluster (Fig. 3f). These results suggest that AVT are effective at aggregating DiI-MBs into a cluster.

The radius of the AVT-trapped DiI-MBs clusters varied from 15 to 40 μm (mean radius: 24.7 μm) (Fig. 3g). The estimated resonance frequency of the DiI-MBs clusters was expected to fall between 70 and 187 kHz, and so 100 kHz was selected as the excitation frequency of US in the subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oscillations and cavitation activity of DiI-MB clusters in response to on-resonance US
ce:section.ce:para: We utilized high-speed microscopy imaging to monitor the dynamic size changes of the DiI-MBs clusters during 100-kHz US excitation at different acoustic pressures (10–65 kPa for 30 cycles). The image sequence and analysis of the radius–time curve revealed that the DiI-MBs clusters remained at rest during the first six frames before the US wavefront reached them. When sonicated at acoustic pressures below 28 kPa (Fig. 4 a, b), the DiI-MBs clusters showed minimal oscillations (≤2 μm; 199.7 ± 185 and 1875.6 ± 1740 μm2/s at 10 and 28 kPa) (Movie S1-S2). At 44 kPa, the DiI-MBs clusters began to exhibit significant expansion and contraction, with radial oscillations reaching 5 μm (19902.8 ± 12006 μm2/s) (Movie S3), while violent oscillations occurred at 58 and 65 kPa (37374.6 ± 24295 and 63243.9 ± 30255 μm2/s, respectively) (Movie S4-S5).

The PCD measurements showed that the AVT-only group barely generated any ICD [(53.6 ± 36) × 103 a.u.] (Fig. 4c). In the 100-kHz US-only group, where DiI-MBs were in a free state, the ICD at 10–58 kPa was very low [(15.6 ± 12.1) × 103 to (573.3 ± 291.4) × 103 a.u.], and only increased slightly at 65 kPa [(746.9 ± 132.4) × 103 a.u.]. For the AVT + 100-kHz US group, the ICD was significantly higher than that for the 100-kHz US-only group, starting from 44 kPa [(2386.6 ± 622.1) × 103 vs. (118.3 ± 113.4) × 103 a.u.], suggesting that the pressure threshold for disrupting DiI-MB clusters was 44 kPa. A pronounced ICD was detected when the acoustic pressure was increased further, [(6330.5 ± 766.5) × 103 and (7914.7 ± 1242.1) × 103 a.u. at 58 and 65 kPa, respectively].

We then used fluorescence high-speed microscopy imaging to evaluate whether the oscillation of the DiI-MBs clusters trapped by AVT with 100-kHz US could release the payload. Fig. 4d demonstrates that the DiI-MB clusters remained unaffected at 10 kPa, since they barely vibrated (Movie S6). At 44 kPa the ejection of fluorescent dye from the DiI-MBs clusters was observed at 250 μs (red arrow), and the size of the cluster was reduced at 500 μs (Movie S7). We speculated that the size reduction of the DiI-MBs cluster was due to most DiI-MBs had released the DiI payload, as evidence of microscopy observations and PCD measurements. At 65 kPa the signal intensity of the DiI-MBs clusters immediately decreased and became blurred at 75 μs, likely due to the violent oscillations of DiI-MBs clusters rapidly release its payload into mist (Movie S8). These results demonstrate that 100-kHz US excitation could induce oscillations in DiI-MB clusters. The oscillatory response of the DiI-MBs clusters showed a positive correlation with acoustic pressure. Furthermore, depending on the oscillatory response of the DiI-MBs clusters, the DiI payload could either be released in a controlled manner or rapidly dispersed into a mist, with minimal disruption to the DiI-MBs clusters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative analyses of payload release from DiI-MB clusters induced by on-resonance US
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm whether the on-resonance US could trigger the release of the payload from the DiI-MBs clusters, we collected fluid samples following 100-kHz US excitation. The fluorescence intensity of the sample was then measured for quantitative analyses. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the payload release efficiency of the AVT-only group was 35.8 ± 7.7 %. The released payloads in the 100-kHz US-only group were significantly lower than in the AVT-only group, but still gradually increased from 2.5 ± 2.6 % at 10 kPa to 11.8 ± 15.1 % at 58 kPa, peaking at 26.0 ± 7.3 % at 65 kPa, suggesting the amount of natural rupture of MBs during the time interval between the 100-kHz US application and the measurement was quite low. In contrast, the AVT + 100-kHz US group demonstrated a payload release efficiency that was markedly higher than in the other groups at each acoustic pressure: 47.3 ± 9.7 %, 66.4 ± 4.9 %, 75.3 ± 5.2 %, and 80.7 ± 11.4 % at 10, 28, 44, and 58 kPa, respectively. When the acoustic pressure was increased to 65 kPa, the payload release efficiency increased further to 93.2 ± 3.0 %, which was similar to that in the sonication-only group.

We then compared the payload release efficiency of AVT + 100-kHz US with other US-based configurations. The quantitative analyses revealed that the AVT + 100-kHz US provided highest payload release efficiency compared to 3-MHz US (off-resonance US) only group (30.6 ± 15.3 %), ARF + 3-MHz US group (73.3 ± 7.9 %), AVT + 3-MHz US group (80.9 ± 11.5 %), and ARF + 100-kHz US group (74.7 ± 78.4 %). These data indicate that our proposed method of AVT trapping combined with US excitation at the resonance frequency of DiI-MBs clusters can generate synergistic effect and improve the efficiency of drug delivery. Furthermore, a strong linear correlation was observed between the ICD and payload release efficiency in the AVT + 100-kHz US group (R 2 = 0.78) (Fig. 5b). indicating the potential of our proposed methodology in monitoring and enhancing the efficiency of drug release.

The findings of this study demonstrate the potential of combining AVT with US at the resonance frequency of MBs clusters (100 kHz) for targeted drug delivery in dynamic environments. The drug release efficiency of nearly 100 % observed in this study represents a significant improvement over ARF methods, making this approach particularly promising for applications in cancer therapy, cardiovascular disease, and other conditions where localized drug delivery is critical. The combination of AVT and on-resonance US offers several advantages over off-resonance US at 3 MHz and ARF-based techniques. The 100-kHz US penetrates deeper into tissues and interacts more effectively with the MBs clusters, which increases the drug release efficiency. Moreover, the use of AVT allows precise control of MBs clustering, which enhances the overall treatment efficacy. On the other hand, the payload release experiment and PCD experiment were conducted under flow conditions with flow velocity of 3 mm/s. The velocities of the blood flow in venules, cerebral capillary network, and skin capillary network may vary within ranges of 0.4–5 mm/s, 0.65–3 mm/s, and 0.5–1.8 mm/s, respectively [10,34–36]. Therefore, it’s possible to apply the proposed AVT in blood flow of cerebral capillary network and skin capillary network.

Numerous studies have shown that applying either simultaneous or sequential dual-frequency US to enhance drug release is generally more effective than single-frequency US treatments [37–39]. Although not explicitly called synergistic effects, these studies often achieve outcomes superior to the simple additive effects of individual treatments. The present study has confirmed that the enhanced payload release observed when combining AVT and US at the resonance frequency of MBs is a synergistic effect. Specifically, combining AVT and 100-kHz US induces significantly more payload release than for each method applied individually. The synergy increased from 14.3 % to 39.7 % as the acoustic pressure of 100-kHz US was increased from 10 to 58 kPa, then leveled off at 34.9 % at 65 kPa, suggesting saturation. The observed synergy between AVT and 100-kHz US may result from a combination of inertial cavitation, microstreaming, shear stress, and other forces. Additionally, the trapping effects of the AVT could compromise the integrity of DiI-MBs, enhancing release when external forces are applied. These findings indicate the potential of optimizing AVT-based drug delivery by integrating dual-frequency US treatments and various pulse sequences to lower the pressure threshold for acoustic disruption [39,40].

Previous studies have demonstrated that AVT can enhance the drug delivery efficiency of MBs in in vitro cellular experiments and in vivo tumor treatments [16,41–43]. However, no previous study has thoroughly investigated the oscillations, cavitation activities, and release of the payload of MBs clusters during on-resonance US excitation. To explore the drug delivery capabilities of MBs clusters, we conducted optical and acoustical measurements concurrently to provide additional analytical perspectives. High-speed microscopy imaging revealed that significant oscillations of individual DiI-MB clusters began at 44 kPa during 100-kHz US excitation. High-speed fluorescence imaging showed a decline in intensity, with signal reductions occurring earlier at higher acoustic pressures, indicating disruption and the release of DiI from the DiI-MBs clusters. Further quantitative experiments demonstrated that the combination of AVT and 100-kHz US at 65 kPa significantly enhanced the payload release efficiency to nearly 100 %, surpassing the efficiency of either method used alone, indicating a synergistic effect. Acoustic analyses revealed that the disruption threshold of MBs clusters was 44 kPa. Additionally, there was a strong correlation between the ICD and payload release efficiency (R 2 = 0.78), suggesting the potential of using the ICD as an indicator for substantial drug release. We found that when ICD signals appeared, the payload release exceeded 80 %.

This study primarily used 100-kHz US as excitation pulses for releasing payloads from AVT-trapped DiI-MB clusters. To assess the impact of US frequency, a 3-MHz US pulse was employed as the excitation pulse for comparison. The results suggest that such off-resonance US could also induce payload release from DiI-MBs clusters. Previous high-speed microscopy imaging showed that US excitation could push DiI-MBs clusters toward the tube walls, gradually disrupting the outer DiI-MBs rather than inducing the whole-cluster disruption observed with 100-kHz US. This may be one of the mechanisms underlying the drug release observed during off-resonance US excitation.

We additionally used ARF in place of AVT as a parallel comparison. The results showed that the payload release efficiency of ARF + 3-MHz US increased with the PRF and exhibited better stability than when using AVT + 3-MHz US, as indicated by smaller standard deviations. This might be attributable to ARF forming multiple smaller DiI-MBs aggregates, which likely allowed for more-uniform payload release than for the larger, multilayer DiI-MB clusters trapped by AVT. On the other hand, when identical acoustic parameters were used for ARF and AVT, the payload release efficiency of ARF + 100 kHz US was only about half (52.5 %) that of AVT + 100 kHz US (100 %). This may have been due to inherent differences between the two types of waveforms. For example, the orbital angular momentum of AVT induces rotations, further increasing the contact of the DiI-MBs clusters with the tube walls. Additionally, the transverse forces applied by AVT to confine the clusters within the trapping region may provide additional interactions during cluster expansion.

Ultrasound energy must be precisely controlled to avoid thermal damage and inertial cavitation. Temperatures above 42 °C coagulate proteins, and above 60 °C cause necrosis [44,45]. Heating increases with higher frequency, pressures > 4000 kPa, and duty cycles > 30 % [46,47]; however, the AVT-concentrated DiI-MBs clusters use much lower settings (165 kPa; 20 % duty), reducing thermal risk. Inertial cavitation, which occurs above 8000 kPa and can produce harmful shock waves [48,49], is also avoided since our pressures (mechanical index: 0.1 for AVT; 0.03–2.0 for on-resonance US) meet FDA guidelines (<1.9) [50,51]. Besides, the spatial-peak temporal-average intensity (ISPTA) for the AVT and the off-resonance ultrasound were 177 mW/cm2 and 0.325 mW/cm2, respectively, still within a range generally considered safe for diagnostic or therapeutic ultrasound by FDA.

While our method effectively accumulates MBs and triggers drug release via AVT and on-resonance US, concerns include potential embolism from MB clusters and cavitation-induced damage. The trapped DiI-MBs clusters (15–40 μm, mean 24.7 μm) are much smaller than rat brain vessels (∼1 mm), and local blood flow autoregulation mitigates blockage risks [52–54]. Moreover, AVT combined with 100-kHz US achieved payload releases of 47–93 % at pressures from 10 to 65 kPa, with disruption of clusters starting at 44 kPa. Notably, minor inertial cavitation may even enhance drug delivery by temporarily increasing endothelial permeability, ensuring effective release with minimal tissue damage. Future work should focus on optimizing the US parameters for specific tissue types and blood flow conditions, as well as conducting in vivo studies to evaluate the long-term safety and efficacy of this methodology. While the results of this study are promising, several challenges remain. Producing MBs with consistent sizes and drug-loading capacities can be difficult, necessitating further optimization to ensure reproducibility. Additionally, the long-term stability of MBs clusters in vivo remains to be fully explored, and more research is needed into how they behave in the complex environment of the human body.

Despite the promising results, a significant challenge in translating this technique to clinical practice is the need for dual-frequency excitation. In our experiments, the frequencies required for acoustic vehicle trapping and drug release exceeded the bandwidth of a single transducer. This necessitated the use of two separate transducers, creating a complex setup that is impractical for clinical applications. To address this issue, alternative transducer designs could be explored. One potential solution is to use an annular array transducer with a low-frequency probe installed coaxially at the center [55]. This design would allow for the simultaneous delivery of both frequencies within a compact form factor suitable for clinical use. Additionally, employing a sparse transducer array that includes elements capable of operating at different frequencies could simplify the hardware requirements and improve feasibility for clinical applications [56]. Further research and development in transducer technology are essential to overcome these challenges and facilitate the clinical translation of this dual-frequency excitation approach.

This study has demonstrated that the combination of AVT and on-resonance US excitation can significantly enhance drug release from MBs clusters, with the release efficiency approaching 93 %. This synergistic effect provides a promising new approach for targeted drug delivery, particularly in conditions where a high blood flow complicates drug accumulation at the treatment site. The concepts demonstrated in this study could be applied to a wide range of medical conditions, including cancer and cardiovascular disease, as well as in gene therapy. The ability to precisely control drug release in dynamic environments opens new possibilities for personalized medicine and targeted therapeutics.

Ching-Hsiang Fan: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Conceptualization. Elaine Huang: Validation, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Wei-Chen Lo: Project administration, Methodology. Chih-Kuang Yeh: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Science and Technology Council, Taiwan (grant number NSTC 111-2221-E-007-019-MY3, 113-2221-E-007-022-MY3, and 113-2923-E-007-004-MY2 to C.K.Y.; NSTC 113-2636-E-006-002 to C.H.F.).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ultsonch.2025.107273.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary video 1
Oscillation dynamics of DiI-MB clusters by bright-field high-speed microscopy imaging during 100-kHz US excitation at 10 kPa.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Low-frequency dual-frequency ultrasound-mediated microbubble cavitation for transdermal minoxidil delivery and hair growth enhancement
